{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks about the location of the special visceral afferent (SVA) fibers of the facial nerve. Special visceral afferent fibers carry taste information from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.

From the documents, particularly Document [18] (Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 7 (Facial) -- Structure and Function), it is stated that the SVA fibers travel with the chorda tympani and enter the brainstem via the nervus intermedius. They terminate at the ipsilateral nucleus of the solitary tract in the rostral medulla.

Document [13] confirms that taste fibers from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue synapse in the nucleus of the tractus solitarius. This is a key point: the SVA fibers of the facial nerve terminate in the nucleus of the tractus solitarius.

Option A (Motor nucleus) is incorrect because it contains motor neurons for facial expression, not sensory afferents.
Option B (Nucleus ambiguous) is involved in efferent (motor) functions, not afferent taste fibers.
Option D (Lacrimatory nucleus) is involved in parasympathetic control of lacrimal glands and is not the site for taste afferents.

Therefore, the correct answer is the nucleus of tractus solitarius.", "answer_choice": "C"}
